author: Writings in Connection with the Donatist Controversy
book: Answer to the Letters of Petilian, the Donatist.

classification.cautionReason: This work discusses heresy in order to refute it; do not classify it as heretical merely from its title.

lineage: Writings in Connection with the Donatist Controversy.
Answer to the Letters of Petilian, the Donatist.
Written in the form of a letter addressed to the Catholics, in which the first portion of the letter which Petilian had written to his adherents is examined and refuted.

originalBook: Written in the form of a letter addressed to the Catholics, in which the first portion of the letter which Petilian had written to his adherents is examined and refuted.
title: Chapter 3

source.title: NPNF1-04. Augustine: The Writings Against the Manichaeans and Against the Donatists

verses.book: Answer to the Letters of Petilian, the Donatist.
verses.text: Chapter 3.—For, so long as they escaped detection, they could not bestow faith on any whom they baptized, but only guilt, if it be true that whosoever receives faith from one that is faithless receives not faith, but guilt.

verses.book: Answer to the Letters of Petilian, the Donatist.
verses.text: Let them therefore be baptized by the good, that they may be enabled to receive not guilt, but faith.

verses.book: Answer to the Letters of Petilian, the Donatist.
verses.text: But how, again, shall they have any certainty about the good who are to give them faith, if what we look to is the conscience of the giver, which is unseen by the eyes of the proposed recipient?

verses.book: Answer to the Letters of Petilian, the Donatist.
verses.text: Therefore, according to their judgment, the salvation of the spirit is made uncertain, so long as in opposition to the holy Scriptures, which say, "It is better to trust in the Lord than to put confidence in man," and, "Cursed be the man that trusteth in man," they remove the hope of those who are to be baptized from the Lord their God, and persuade them that it should be placed in man; the practical result of which is, that their salvation becomes not merely uncertain, but actually null and void.

verses.book: Answer to the Letters of Petilian, the Donatist.
verses.text: For "salvation belongeth unto the Lord," and "vain is the help of man."

verses.book: Answer to the Letters of Petilian, the Donatist.
verses.text: Therefore, whosoever places his trust in man, even in one whom he knows to be just and innocent, is accursed.

verses.book: Answer to the Letters of Petilian, the Donatist.
verses.text: Whence also the Apostle Paul finds fault with those who said they were of Paul saying, "Was Paul crucified for you? or were ye baptized in the name of Paul?"
